comments.title: Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Thank you, as ever, for hosting these Magic Mondays. This week I have three questions for you regarding the FHR.

1. I am currently working on the opening/closing of the Temple and I was wondering if there were any specific visualizations involved in the purification of the Temple by the four Elements.

I am asking because I had some spontaneous mental imagery while clearing and cleansing the Temple according to the ancient ways.

After walking with the fan around the Temple, in my imagination a faint yellow(ish) glow lingered around the fan when I took it back to the altar. When I took the fan from the altar again to clear and cleanse the Temple a second time during the Closing, I sensed the yellow glow was still there. So after walking around the Temple with the fan it seemed to make sense to raise the fan again to the east to send the energy back to its abode, and I saw the glow vanish. (This applied to all four elements, btw, not only to Air.)

To put it differently, I felt that in the Opening the energies of the elements were invoked into each symbol prior to walking around the temple, while in the Closing the same energies were banished from the symbols subsequent to walking around the temple.

My question: is such a mental imagery appropriate? I’m still at the beginning of my practice of the opening/closing ceremony, so I’d like to know if this makes sense before giving it a chance to stick around, i.e. to become a "stable" part of my experience with the ceremony. (And if you think this is but nitpicking, by all means let me know and I’ll make sure I don’t bother you again with petty details.)

2. Speaking of Opening and Closing, I don’t recall seeing a Temple form of that ceremony, i.e. with all Temple officers, in the four FHR volumes. In other words, I now know the ritual procedure to open and close a Temple of the FHR on my own, but I’m not sure how exactly this should be done if I were together with other officers.

This said, I realize I’m not anywhere near needing such a ceremony, as I am a Candidate working on my own, but I thought I should ask, for the sake of completeness.

3. In your discussion of numerology last year you noted that many readers of the commentariat with birth number 8 found that the description did not fit them, which led you to conclude that the description might need some updating. Did you have a chance to gather sufficient information to revise your description of birth number 8?

Thank you for your time, and sorry if I'm asking too much after the intense weekend you just had!

R.

comments.title: Potency of Latin mass
comments.content: You’ve mentioned before how the Latin Mass of the Roman Catholic Church had a magical potency to it that was lost when it was changed to English. Can you please briefly recap the reasons for this, or link to the original post? I wanted to share with a Catholic friend, but I’ve searched for your explanation and can’t find it.

replies.title: Re: Potency of Latin mass
replies.content:
Obviously not JMG, but there are two main explanations:

From an outsider's perspective, the Catholic Church painted itself into a corner in its theology of ritual and started to lose its institutional knowledge of how to intelligently construct and operate a rite. To be perfectly blunt, the people who composed the Tridentine mass knew what they were doing. The people who composed the Novus Ordo didn't. If you are willing to throw around particularly nasty allegations, it may also be that the invocation of benefic divine forces may have caused discomfort in some members of the church hierarchy, so they intentionally watered things down to accommodate their own lack of piety.

From an insider's perspective, two very strange things happened at the council of Trent. First, the full spiritual authority and force of the catholic church were brought to bear to make a binding resolution that anyone who tries to translate the mass into vernacular is an excommunicate heretic. The second is that it was the first ecumenical council to include both a bible and a copy of canon law on the altar, instead of just a bible. The second Vatican council never repealed the canons from the council of Trent, but they did try to translate the mass into vernacular. By their own standards, that would make the entire Catholic Church hierarchy excommunicate. Secondly, binding themselves under the auspices of the Bible includes provisions for both screwing up and being forgiven. The canon law has no such provisions, there are rules, and there are punishments. After Trent, lapses and failures that were once part of the normal process of coming back to god's grace became explicit failures and corruptions of a specific legal code. The Catholic Church has, by its own standards, basically succeeded in shooting itself through all three feet and all four cheeks.

replies.title: Re: Potency of Latin mass
replies.content:
The problem with the current Catholic Mass isn't that it's been translated into the vernacular. The problem is that the committee that created it rewrote the Mass in ways that violated more than a millennium of church teaching and tradition, and gutted the spiritual dimensions of the ceremony. The new mass is called the Novus Ordo in Latin, and you can find reams of good theological critique if you're interested. Here are a few good discussions:

http://archives.sspx.org/SSPX_FAQs/q5_novus_ordo_missae.htm
https://onepeterfive.com/why-do-people-have-a-problem-with-the-novus-ordo/
https://unavocecanada.org/wp-content/uploads/2017/04/ottaviani-intervention.pdf

replies.title: Re: Potency of Latin mass
replies.content:
As an aside to this, two questions came to mind.

1. Given the old rituals had a more potent power, I'm guessing that current catholics would find greater benefit attending the Traditional Latin Mass and utilizing pre vatican 2 prayers/practices?

2. This is for the commentariat. How is the Orthodox Liturgy in comparison, for those who've attended it,to the catholic services (old and new)?

Tamanous

replies.title: Re: Potency of Latin mass
replies.content: 1. I've been to both traditional and Novus Ordo parishes.  The effectiveness of NO sacraments is minimal.  What I experienced in receiving the traditional forms of the sacraments was mind-blowingly powerful.  It seems as though there are at least two Jesii operating under the banner of Roman Catholicism: a very powerful and active one in the traditionalist movement, and one that might as well as be a purely human-created and driven egregore.

replies.title: Re: Potency of Latin mass
replies.content: 1) I know several former Catholics who dropped out after the Novus Ordo mass came into general use. They all say that the power went right out of it, and the ceremony became an empty shell of itself. Draw your own conclusions.

replies.title: Re: Potency of Latin mass
replies.content:
Many years ago, it may have been before Vatican II, a friend gave me some Rosicrucian literature to read. According to this reading the Rosicrucians predicted the downfall of the Roman Catholic Church if the mass was performed in the vernacular. If I recall correctly, they posited that the repetition throughout the world of the same words in the same language created a powerful force. I don't recall what they labeled this force.

It just occurred to me that in Scriptural terms the Latin Mass recreated the condition of humankind before the Tower of Babal, with the Church and its sacraments serving as a replacement for the tower that was intended to reach the heavens. God destroyed the original tower because it was the product of human arrogance, thinking they could do it on their own. But since Jesus was commissioned to create a path to heaven for humankind the Church and the sacraments he is believed to have instituted could renew the attempt with God's blessing. So Vatican II essentially repeated the original curse of dividing humans through language. I wonder whether this notion that popped into my mind is actually present somewhere in Catholic theology.

Rita

replies.title: Re: Potency of Latin mass
replies.content:
One intriguing factor I see is that prior to the Council of Trent, the mass, ordination rituals, etc were all different in different places; there was an enormous amount of variation in all of the rites of Medieval Catholicism. There wasn't even a single official version of the bible either, with debate occurring about which books to include, which language to use, etc. Then, the Council of Trent decided to try to codify things, including the ritual forms of the church, and in symbolic terms I suppose it could be said that they tried to recreate the Tower of Babel.

I wonder if the radical reduction in the power and authority of the Church around this time, and the way it seems to have stepped from one self-imposed disaster to another since then, is related...

replies.title: Re: Potency of Latin mass
replies.content:
The change to the Novus Ordo was much more than a change in language (from Latin to vernacular). To my outsider's eyes, the Novus Ordo feels very heavily "Protestantized." (An ordinary Protestant Sunday service has in it a good deal of the schoolroom, instructing the congregation and building community among them far more than enabling them to approach the ineffable mysteries of the Divine.) And, when compared to the magnificent Byzantine Sunday liturgy in even a small parish church, even the Roman Catholic Vetus Ordo (the old Latin Mass) looks like a marked simplification of how the early church once to celebrated the Eucharist.

Interestingly enough, at least in the Slavonic version of Orthodoxy, the building where the liturgy is celebrated is formally called a Temple (Храм), not a Church (Церковь). The extreme conservatism of the Byzantine ritual practice makes it a likely place to look for remnants of our host's Temple Tradition, too.

As for the use of Latin vs. vernaculars in worship: Liturgical Latin is what is technically called a "sacral language," that is, a formalized language different chiefly in its style and vocabulary from any and every vernacular language. Sacral languages are a world-wide cultural phenomenon, and they do not need writing. Traditional aboriginal cultures that did not use writing very often (always?) have them, too. Hardly anywhere except in the modern Western world do people use their every-day, informal vernaculars when they deal with the Sacred. This seems to be hard-wired into the human organism.

(Sacral languages are used even outside of a strictly religious context, for example, sacral English in the rituals of Freemasonry.)

There is a great deal of scholarly work on sacral languages. For the Latin case, I would particularly recommend Christine Mohrmann's brief book, Liturgical Latin: Its Origins and Character (1957), which you can download or read on archive.org.

comments.title: Good Karma
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Please forgive me for asking what may appear to be a very basic question, but... what is good karma, exactly, and how may one generate it?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Good Karma
replies.content:
Dear Winterknight,

While this may appear to be a basic question, it really isn't. One of the open theological debates in both Buddhist and Hindu traditions is whether or not "good" karma even exists. The two main positions on the issue being that since karma is cause and effect, speaking of good and bad karma is about as reasonable as good or bad gravity. The other main position is that while karma in itself is neutral, it can give rise to effects that are helpful, harmful, or indifferent to one's spiritual development. In this view, "good karma" is karma that is conducive to one's spiritual path.

By convention, good karma is generated by doing good. The precise meaning and formulaic description, of course, varies from one system of practice to another. Abstaining from harming others, acting for the welfare of others, cultivating ones virtues and attenuating ones vices, is one such formulation.

replies.title: Re: Good Karma
replies.content:
Pretty much what Anon said, but a few additional notes might be worth putting in.

The labels "good karma" and "bad karma" are very blunt instruments. Every one of your thoughts, words, and deeds creates consequences, and those consequences shape who you are and what you can expect in this and future lives. If you're okay with the consequences, they can be called "good karma" in a broad sense, but it's not simply a matter of totting up points on one side or the other.

You generate favorable karma by thoughts, words, and deeds that have consequences that further your spiritual development and the happiness of others. Broad, isn't it? That's just it, of course -- in any given situation there are countless helpful things you can do, and even more harmful things you can refrain from doing, and every one of those has a different set of consequences and thus a different karmic result. It's rarely possible to figure out in advance what actions will create what karma. Thus it's more useful simply to follow the ordinary precepts of a common sense morality, behave with kindness, courtesy, and self-discipline, and let the Divine take care of the rest.

I light candles for the planetary deities of the days of the week, and for the seasonal rituals of the wheel of the year. I have a small box of brown candles, bought before I had firmly established this routine. I have been tidying my candle and incense drawer and I wondered what to do with them. During the seasonal rituals I use green for earth. The only time when brown might feel right is Samhain, when it is dark and the light is not yet increasing, but at each festival during the darker half of the year I ask which colour to use and always intuit green.

What are other classical, beneficial ritual uses for brown candles?

replies.title: Re: Candle colours: brown
replies.content: There aren't any. Brown wasn't used in traditional color symbolism, since it's basically a muddle of colors. You might consider keeping them for power outages.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Sometimes my job brings in a crew of guys from a halfway house to help out, but we don't make a big deal since they are ex cons and drug addicts trying to get back on their feet. One of the guys has brain damage and says things that don't make any sense, but they give him simple tasks where he stays out of the way. By the scars, tattoos, and flaps of loose skin where he used to be obese it's obvious he used to be very different. I'm not sure what caused his transformation but he's now a mostly gentle person and most of his co-workers either ignore him or push him aside when it's time to get something done.

Studying the DRHM has has more a profound effect on me, because I started to listen to what he was saying and responding trying to be polite and then began to notice patterns. He talks in a metaphor language, and while some of it I cannot interpret other parts were astonishingly synchronous or even occult in nature. We had the most wonderful crazy conversations and he really enjoyed it, even though everyone else who overheard must have been totally confused about what was going on. I was very careful to respond in a way that didn't betray my occult hobby to the eavesdroppers, but he seemed to be able to process the answers and respond in a legitimate exchange of ideas.

My guess is that an astral filter that we all normally have is broken. He was very fond of bible versus and struggling with binary thinking because it wasn't lining up with his lived experience, and he seemed grateful that I brought in different perspectives from other religions and traditions that introduced the tertiary concept. He also revealed some things to me, and it seemed like something was speaking through him trying to deliver messages, or he was mentioning reflections that came up in the light.

I'm not sure how all this works, but it was fascinating. I'm curious if you've run into this sort of thing before and have and idea about the mechanics behind it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
No, I haven't encountered that before, but I've read that this is fairly common among some people who suffer various kinds of neurological or psychological crises. The thing that fascinates me most is that one of H.P. Lovecraft's odder stories, "Beyond the Wall of Sleep," is about the same sort of thing...

https://hplovecraft.com/writings/texts/fiction/bws.aspx

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"Glaze fishily" is my new favorite phrase.
Not sure where to drop it but I'll find an excuse

Now I wish I could have listened better but work was very busy. I gave him my phone number but he's probably not capable of using it. Maybe next time.

There was definitely a struggle with the remnants of his old violent personality which came up in moments of frustration with his coworkers or his inability to talk with women without making them uncomfortable, but he seemed incapable of acting out on those tendencies and they retreated when we discussed philosophy. The effect of our conversations taxed my ability to think in concrete terms, and it was difficult to transition my attention back to my job duties. I had to ask a coworker to tell me what to do towards the end of the shift because I developed a case of brain freeze, but they laughed and obliged and everything went smooth after that.

The Lovecraft story also highlighted the sense of familiarity which is what grabbed my attention at first. Whoever or whatever it was seemed to know things about me, making me wish I took notes instead of trying to remember it all.

Thanks for sharing it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I do that sometimes when I encounter certain kinds of crazy people, and then I feel ambivalent about it because maybe I should have been putting whichever spirits that had a finger in the pie under a different set of incentives, or I'm being an enabler, or I might have had an accumulation of stray intentions and anticipation-habits that was conveniently using the randomness of their behavior as the path of least resistance in a way that hurt them, or something. Often some side circumstance comes up to terminate the conversation just when I would have begun to get uncomfortable or get too entangled, that imposes some slightly enduring personal hardship on them (e.g. getting ejected from a bookstore), and I wonder if I should have drawn some boundary myself sooner to spare them that trouble.

I guess whatever spirits may have had a hand in your interlocutor's behavior might have been less malefically unbalanced than what I see in most of the people I run into, so you wouldn't have had as much of a problem.

replies.content:
I've seen a good case made that the trilogy is partly sort of a coded artistic allegory for the experience of the process of discovering that one is trans, without already having had adequate concepts of what that even was ("nobody can tell you what the Matrix is"), and then coming to terms with how that puts one in conflict with conservative or unreflective society. Some segments of dialogue that otherwise seem to be talking around things pointlessly or making weird emphases on seemingly irrelevant stuff make more sense this way. Trinity (in room 303 of the heart) is supposed to represent Neo's trans identity and self-actualized unification of a previously disunited self (in room 101). Red pills stand in for the MTF hormone replacement pills, which were red at the time. The Merovingian (also in room 101 of a different building) represents a trans person who decided to stay closeted for the sake of retaining social power and influence, and is angry and jealous of what Neo has. Smith always emphasizes "Mister" when talking to Neo and never says "Neo" except outside of his earshot, all the agents are cis white men, Neo flying around and incidentally breaking oppressive reflective glass is supposed to represent the shockwaves and disruption of identity-discordant social feedback that a transitioned person going around in public can't help but make, the spinny floating martial arts moves in a long coat are supposed to be reflective of the MTF experience of a raw experience of liberation from observing oneself spinning a dress, everyone who stays plugged into the Matrix (gender binary preconceptions and automatic normativities around them) is supporting it, etc.

Of course, sufficiently charged and myth-like allegories have the weird quality that people can easily relate them to other things with other meanings as well.

comments.title: Good, honest dirt
comments.content:
Apropos to your general maxim of not monkeying with established systems and traditions, I'll risk a grumpy admonition to consider remedial reading classes and ask the following...

What do you predict the result would be if one practiced the classic Golden Dawn system, but making the single change of banishing Spirit in the LBRP rather than Earth? I have a friend who is considering magic, and would be most comfortable with using the Judeo-Christian divine names and symbolism (indeed would be uncomfortable with anything else), but like me and many others, just cannot bring herself to blame the earth (and the element thereof) for all the evil and imbalance in existence. (Think of, for instance, the gospel of John; the Logos created the world, therefore it is extremely nifty.)

Do you foresee that spoiling the egregore altogether and causing lousy or no results, or worse, causing trouble or harm to the practitioner? If so, what would be your advice for someone in my friend's situation? I thought about her using one of the SOP-based systems with the J-X elements plugged in ("By the Great archangel Raphael, and in the Great Name YHWH, I call upon the element of Air, its Spirits and its Powers.." etc.) I was just curious if, since you've made this change in all the alternative versions of the LBRP you've developed, you thought in the case of the classic GD system this might be an acceptable exception to the rule.

replies.title: Re: Good, honest dirt
replies.content: Nope. The GD system is what it is, and it uses the earth banishing for a very carefully chosen series of reasons. If you haven't yet worked your way through a magical system you do not have the necessary knowledge and skill to make changes with it. These days, however, a lot of people insist on making such changes; it's a way of refusing to learn anything new, and by and large, it succeeds in its purpose. If you or your friend want to do classic GD magic, do classic GD magic -- don't piss in your coffee under the mistaken impression that this will make it taste better.

replies.title: Re: Good, honest dirt
replies.content: Not the OP, but I'm curious about this as well, and would like to understand why it's done this way. Is there a good resource which would help me understand why I'm banishing the element of Earth daily?

replies.title: Re: Good, honest dirt
replies.content: Because most of us have an excess focus on the purely material plane, and one focus of the GD system is moving from such a focus to a more balanced one that embraces the spiritual as well as the physical.

replies.title: Re: Good, honest dirt
replies.content:
"...don't piss in your coffee under the mistaken impression that this will make it taste better."

And... the lack of a readily available beverage was the sole factor contributing to the salvation of my keyboard!

Fair enough--asked and answered! If she shows any further interest in pursuing occultism, I'll perhaps have her take a look at classic GD as written and compare it to, say, the FotHR system using archangels and Hebrew names and see which one she's drawn towards. As for me, I'm working my way down the CGD path and loving it!

replies.title: Re: Geomancy reading
